description:
You are acting as a data-analyst who needs a quick, text-based snapshot of how much space a small set of CSV files is taking on disk.

    1. Inside the home directory there is a folder named “/home/user/data/csv/”.  
       It already contains exactly three files:
           • marketing.csv  
           • sales_q1.csv  
           • sales_q2.csv  

    2. Compute the exact, **apparent** byte size of every file (i.e. the number that “stat -c%s FILE” would return, not the on-disk block allocation).

    3. Convert every byte value to a human-readable form that follows the IEC-style
       abbreviations produced by the GNU coreutils program “numfmt --to=iec”.  
       (Example: 10240 becomes 10K, 30720 becomes 30K, and so on.)

    4. Create a directory “/home/user/reports/” (if it does not already exist) and
       write a new, comma-separated report called

           /home/user/reports/csv_disk_usage_report.txt

       The log file must contain **exactly four lines** in this order:

           a) One line per CSV file, sorted alphabetically by file name.  
           b) A final summary line that begins with the literal word “TOTAL”.

       The required format for every line is:

           <filename>,<size_in_bytes>,<size_human>

       For example (this is the precise layout the automatic checker will look for,
       including the commas and no extra spaces):

           marketing.csv,30720,30K
           sales_q1.csv,10240,10K
           sales_q2.csv,20480,20K
           TOTAL,61440,60K

       • The “filename” column must contain only the base name (no path).  
       • The “size_human” column must be the value produced by
         “numfmt --to=iec --format='%.0f'”.

    5. Make sure that “csv_disk_usage_report.txt” ends with a trailing newline
       (usual POSIX text-file convention).

    Finish the task when the file exists, has the exact four lines shown above, and can be viewed with “cat /home/user/reports/csv_disk_usage_report.txt”.
truth:
Pre-existing directory structure and file contents/sizes at task start:

    1. Directory: /home/user/data/csv/

    2. Files (plain text CSV, contents unimportant for the task but sizes are
       fixed and **must not change** during test setup):

       • /home/user/data/csv/marketing.csv   –  30 720 bytes (0x7800)
       • /home/user/data/csv/sales_q1.csv    –  10 240 bytes (0x2800)
       • /home/user/data/csv/sales_q2.csv    –  20 480 bytes (0x5000)

       The harness will create these files with exactly those byte counts so
       “stat -c%s” returns 30720, 10240 and 20480 respectively.

    3. The directory /home/user/reports/ does **not** exist when the agent starts;
       the agent must create it.

    Expected artefact created by the agent:

    File: /home/user/reports/csv_disk_usage_report.txt  
    Permissions: 0644 (rw-r--r--) owned by the regular user.  
    Contents (including final newline):

        marketing.csv,30720,30K
        sales_q1.csv,10240,10K
        sales_q2.csv,20480,20K
        TOTAL,61440,60K

    The grading script will:
        • Verify the file exists at the exact path.  
        • Check it has exactly 4 lines (wc -l == 4).  
        • Compare the entire file content (sha256) to the expected text above.  
        • Ensure each CSV’s apparent size matches those listed above by running
          “stat -c%s” on the originals.

    Note: the agent is not allowed to hard-code the answer; it must derive the
    numbers from the actual files, but the autograder will only look for the
    final file and its content, not the intermediary commands used.
name: task_000000_e51eb389
